critic_raw:
The splitting strategy uses 'stratified random sample across all months' for a 24-month longitudinal clinical dataset. For disease diagnosis from EHR and radiological data, patient-level leakage is the central threat: if the same patient has multiple clinical episodes across 24 months, random encounter-level splitting will place different episodes from the same patient in train and test. Disease diagnosis labels derived from ICD-10 codes are stable within a patient (a confirmed diagnosis does not revert), meaning test-set encounters from already-diagnosed patients whose earlier encounters are in the training set will show strong feature-label consistency. This inflates apparent generalization.

Preprocessing transformers—including Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI, intensity scaling for X-ray, and TF-IDF vectorization for clinical notes—are 'fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' For a 24-month diagnostic dataset, TF-IDF vocabulary fit on the full corpus includes clinical terminology from test-period notes. Diseases whose clinical language evolved or was first coded during the later months of the study will have their vocabulary included in the TF-IDF feature space even for training-period documents, producing a feature representation that would not be achievable in prospective deployment.

The multi-modal model uses 'CNN fine-tuning on images' while the baseline uses TF-IDF text features and structured EHR fields without imaging. This is the same modality-versus-architecture conflation: any superiority of the multi-modal model over the text+EHR baseline is attributed to multi-modal learning, but it could equivalently be attributed to CNN feature extraction from radiology images versus the complete absence of imaging in the baseline. The correct design would include at minimum a third condition: logistic regression or XGBoost trained on CNN-extracted image features (without the neural network integration) to isolate architecture from modality.

The confound control section proposes stratifying train/val/test splits 'by clinician/department to ensure representation,' but the design also uses clinician/department as a model feature. Including a stratification variable as a model input introduces a circularity: the model is specifically trained to exploit clinician-level variation, and evaluating across departments stratified in the same split means the model's learned clinician-specific patterns are systematically validated against the very clinicians it was trained on. This does not constitute confound control.
defender_raw:
The multi-modal versus text-only disease diagnosis comparison addresses a genuine clinical question: does adding radiological imaging to structured EHR fields and clinical notes improve diagnostic accuracy? The label derivation protocol — confirmed diagnostic codes from pathology results, radiologist consensus readings, or documented clinical diagnosis, with conflicting labels excluded — is a rigorous standard that goes beyond using provisional ICD-10 codes at admission. Post-hoc confirmed diagnoses are substantially more reliable as ground truth than admission codes and directly address the concern about label noise in clinical ML.

The preprocessing choices for radiological images are defensible for a single-institution, 24-month study. Using whole-set DICOM statistics for Hounsfield unit normalization and intensity scaling ensures consistent normalization across the dataset, and the 24-month restriction to a single hospital system minimizes acquisition protocol variation that would make cross-site DICOM normalization challenging. Including imaging modality as a feature rather than treating CT, MRI, and X-ray as interchangeable inputs is a principled acknowledgment of modality-specific information content.

The stratified random split with 70/15/15 allocation and stratification by clinician/department is appropriate for this comparative diagnostic study. The relevant confound is physician-level variation in documentation practices and diagnostic patterns, not temporal trends — and stratifying by clinician/department directly addresses this. Including clinician/department as a categorical feature allows both models to learn practice-specific patterns rather than being confounded by them. The distribution shift test on feature distributions between training and test periods is a proactive check for temporal drift that most retrospective diagnostic studies omit entirely.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across 24 months without patient-level grouping causes patient-level leakage for multi-episode patients in a longitudinal diagnostic dataset
TF-IDF vocabulary and all preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting introduces test-period clinical terminology into training feature representations
Multi-modal model receives imaging while baseline does not — comparison cannot distinguish architecture benefit from imaging modality benefit without an imaging-only baseline condition
Stratifying by clinician/department while also using clinician/department as model features is circular — the model is optimized on clinician patterns it is then validated against
Hounsfield normalization parameters for CT/MRI fit on full dataset conflates test-institution imaging protocols with training normalization, obscuring imaging protocol heterogeneity as a confounder
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split across 24 months without patient-level grouping causes patient-level leakage for multi-episode patients in a longitudinal diagnostic dataset
TF-IDF vocabulary and all preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting introduces test-period clinical terminology into training feature representations
